title: Sickle Cell Crisis - Vaso-Occlusive with Acute Chest Syndrome Risk

patient_data.chief_complaint: Severe bilateral leg and back pain for 2 days, now with fever and mild chest pain
symptoms: Severe bilateral lower extremity pain (bones/joints) — 9/10 on pain scale
Pain unresponsive to oral oxycodone at home
New onset mild right-sided chest pain with shallow breathing
patient_data.history: Sickle cell disease (HbSS) diagnosed at birth. 3-4 VOC crises per year. Last crisis 2 months ago. Previous acute chest syndrome episode (age 18). Avascular necrosis bilateral hips. On hydroxyurea — admits missing doses recently. No surgical history

lab_results.CXR: New infiltrate right lower lobe (developing acute chest syndrome)

expected_output.primary_diagnosis: Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis with Evolving Acute Chest Syndrome
should_detect: Aggressive pain management: IV morphine PCA or hydromorphone (NEVER meperidine — allergy/seizure risk in SCD)
Exchange transfusion consideration (Hb 6.2 + acute chest syndrome developing)
Phenotype-matched blood (anti-C, anti-E antibodies — extended crossmatch needed)
Simple transfusion if exchange not immediately available (target Hb 10, HbS <30%)
Incentive spirometry q2h (prevents atelectasis → ACS progression)
Empiric antibiotics for acute chest syndrome: ceftriaxone + azithromycin
IV fluid hydration (cautious — avoid overhydration which worsens ACS)
Reinforce hydroxyurea compliance (disease-modifying, reduces VOC frequency)
Hematology consult for long-term management: consider crizanlizumab or L-glutamine
red_flags: Acute chest syndrome evolving (new CXR infiltrate + chest pain + fever)
